words: It was $280, I think. It was a lot of farm ground on it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Not any timber, merchantable timber. There was lots of brush land left. There were 125 acres cleared and the balance was brush. Pasture.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: It was a good prospect. If we could have just got another two bits of moisture for wheat, fine. We couldn't get it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there many other people that were running sheep right in the area at the time?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: There were quite a few little 10 and 15 head bands, yes. But there weren't any bands around me. Well, there was two. Bob Thompson had 100. Well, there were one or two bands around of 100 or better.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Most people didn't like sheep. Most of them didn't. I figured I made it good for the sheep herders. Kept out competition. Left you more room to pasture and bucked you this and that.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But I was lucky. On those sheep, I bought them for a dollar and a half a head. I had about a lamb and a half to sell the next fall from HU. And I got,

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I think I got about $5 for lamb.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Or was that a little heavy?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I think I got around 6 cents a pound. Some were close. And sold the damn ewes for $3 .50 after selling the lamb crop. Sheep always did treat me that way. Maybe it's money a man could make.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you run him in the outside of your place back in the countryside there?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Lee Burchier came up and said to me that if I would tend camp for him, that he would take these sheep of mine and put them with his. Because he had 500 and I had 100 ewes, they were a little better. And we took them to the range. And we started them up really quick and fed that all out. And then we crossed over into the head of Bear Creek and come in there behind White Corries and finished up there at White Corries in the fall. And what I did, I'd take him to groceries and his mail and salt and everything he needed and move his camp twice a week.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And he did the herding.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And it worked very good for both of us.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: We had a little bear trouble. We lost a few lambs, but not too many. Between us, probably 15 or 20. Bears get into them, but we got about half as many bears as they got lambs.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: We trapped them. Get the dogs starved down. We had a bunch of pretty good dogs. They'd chase them and tree them and we'd get them that way. We had some fun. Some excitement.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And a scare or two.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Of the bears?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah. Yeah. One time there was a great big old yellow pine tree snagged laying up and down a steep hillside. And there was a bear that we were after stepped up on the top of that and stood there broadside and Lee shot him. And he kicked. He went on this side. He went on this side. He went on this side. And he come right at us. We were standing at the stump. Lee said, we'd probably better get the hell out of here. We took off and pretty soon that bear kicked right down into the creek and deader than I would do in hell. But that reaction after he was shot, it was just funny. He just crossed back and forth over that log and acted just like he was alive. But he was coming right towards us. So we got out of there.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: It sounds like a pretty good arrangement to have with this guy. It looked that way to us, yes. Oh, I was saying the arrangement you had with this guy for him to do the herding and you to supply him. That sounds like a good deal. It was. It was a good deal for both of us. Because he didn't have horses or equipment to get his stuff. And other than that, he couldn't leave the sheep long enough to go and get it. It didn't take me very long. A couple of times a week to go and supply him and move his camp and set him up.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I made him kind of peeved one day. I didn't get into camp to move him quick enough. He thought it wasn't quick enough. It was almost dark. It was dark by the time I got through.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: He had packed everything. But he got his supper and he figured it was too late and I wouldn't be there.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: So I know he had a can of tomatoes and he only ate half of them. So he had them in a dish in this box. And he had a lot of stuff. It shouldn't have been packed. Wouldn't have ordinarily. But I put that on that horse, the box on each side, and led him over quite a steep hill and down into another draw. Unpacked it and about the time I got the tent set up and his bed roll packed into the tent and his stove set up, here come Lee. And he was kind of peeved. A little more than peeved. Mattering hell. He says, you moved that stuff and he says, I didn't figure on your company. I didn't get that ready to move. He says, there's tomatoes in there and there's a jar of sourdough and there's no lid on it. He says, that box of stuff is, I'll bet you that everything in it is ruined. Boy, I felt pretty bad. He took the lid off, took the tomatoes out. I don't think he spilled a drop. And he went on down and the sourdough I didn't think he spilled a drop. He was happy and I felt better.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But I did try to move him earlier than that, after that. I don't know what held me up that day, but something, something held me back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he a bachelor? Was he a bachelor?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah. Well, no. Let's see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: His wife lived in Lewiston and he owned a place in Lewiston.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But none of them ever come up there.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I pulled over and employed and they didn't, they didn't know, he was just a starting. He wasn't, he wasn't out of the woods yet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, you have grazing rights in the area there?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah. No. I had the whole area all Ruby Creek, Flat Creek, Bear Creek. We had grazing rights on 4000 acres, I expect.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you got grazing rights just by going in with him then? Is that all that?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah. Mm hm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you keep the partnership up for very long?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No, just the one year. He, I had to sell out, well I was, I had to get out because we were coming here. So I sold a she.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I, I sold every one of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You kept some? No, I, I, that bunch of things, I sold every one of them, didn't I? You kept that big old long thing that high and long hair on her about so?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well didn't I go to, yes I got, I must have kept, I must have kept them ewes that I got at WSC. I got 13 head of ewes from WSC. And I guess we kept them. But I sold everything else.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Okay, um, this is fun. You know, it seemed to me like your roots in a way are over on the other side of the mountain more than here. I've had the feeling that, you know, that your, because your oldest friends are over there and those are people you grew up with. As compared to the people over here where you're more of a newcomer, you know. And I've kind of wondered how the difference was between the neighborhood over there and the neighborhood over here. It seemed like that was a more solid, old time neighborhood than what you came to when you moved over here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that it?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: It was, overall. But down here on Allen's place, the Pelphrey was a newcomer. Up here on Bountains place, Bountains were newcomers. And over on Davis's, the next place over, no the second place over, was another newcomer. And we just worked right together because we were newcomers. There were some of the older people who accepted us, showstooms and osmonds and the cleans over here. But as a rule,

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: it's pretty hard to break into them as a, it was hard to get acquainted with them.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But, on account of having all these newcomers, it made it a lot easier. We exchanged work, we exchanged visits, we helped one another just like friends would. Sometimes we ate together, sometimes harvested together or butchered or things like that. And just got by pretty good. But I'm sure glad they were there because the old time neighborhood people didn't share whether they accepted us. They just wouldn't.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: They were kind of hard to break into. Not all of them. Not all of them, but as a rule that was true. One new businessman in Troy says it took me five years to get these people in this neighborhood to even trade with me. As they should, all of them. He says there would always be a few, but he says it never, it took five years to get started, acquainted, or whatever you call it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he Swedish or Norwegian? Was he Scandinavian?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah, he was. He was Swedish, but the neighborhood, he was a stranger. And they had their habits. They traded in the store. They'd been trading there for fifty years and they wouldn't change. He would know. And the same thing kind of applied to us.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I don't understand it, but it was hard to break in to one group. And on the other hand, they elected me on the school board the first year I was here. The neighborhood was trying.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And all of them. And we got by, we learned to like these people and they finally learned to like us. But they weren't real anxious to cross that barrier between a stranger and somebody they knew. What they finally did...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which school district was the one here?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: This district here, although there was a school right down here at Mike Redeens, we belonged to this district over here at Big Meadow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And where was the school?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: The school was over at Big Meadow where the church is. That church is the old original school house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Big Meadow, now where are we talking about exactly? Which church is that?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well,

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: it's on Walt Lillard's place.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I think you've seen that Advent church over there. That's where the school was. But these kids never had to go over there. They had consolidated.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And our kids found a bus right here in front of the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did it come that there was a school?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: There was a school some other place. They brought that over when they bought that church. They brought that church over there from some place up here on the hill. I don't know for sure where.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which board were you on?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Big Meadow over there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was that?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: They wanted something done.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They put you on the board, you mean?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: They wanted something done and they were afraid somebody else wouldn't do it. I don't know. I don't know. They were consolidated. And yet they had their individual school board out here. We had to go to town to school board meetings that were represented from White Pine over here, Big Meadow, down here at Nelson's. And a school board meeting at that time would involve, I don't know, 25 or 30 people. And arguments, oh boy. Still have them in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was an argument over then?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh, sours, insurance, bus drivers, repair on school buildings in Troy. Some people go right along with it and other people said they let them fix their own damn school. But we finally wound up going in and helping them donate labor to finish those buildings up.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And finally they just eliminated all these out here and had a three -man school board. Instead of having a dozen schools around the country representing them, they just had a three -man school board. And that took two or three years to get that.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: You know, when you talk about, and hard to break into this community, when we talked about what it was like for newcomers around Harvard, you said that it was pretty easy for them. That they were pretty well accepted. They were. So there was a difference between there and here.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: There was every nationality under the sun over there, but there were no Japanese up there.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And when we first went in there, and until we left even, there was a constant change in the population for different reasons. And when we came over here, these people were all born here.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And there hadn't been any newcomers come into the area until about the time we started, we came. And there was, at the last of that depression, these people around here were, oh, they wanted to get out of here and go someplace else. Well, the war started soon after we came here, and they all, or anybody that could get away, went to the coast to the shipyards and wore whatever happened to be. They wanted to get out, they could get out and make big money working. But it was almost impossible to make a living farming. You could make enough to eat and that was about all. But that condition changed with the war, and then a few of us worked and done our farm work and still went out and got a job, saw milling or logging or going to Moscow in construction or anything.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I can remember when my brother, Oscar, was working in the shipyard in Bermondton and made a dollar an hour. Boy, I thought that was magnificent to what we had been making. It was for a while until prices started going up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were for, like, steel smiths, what kind of pay did you get doing that kind of work?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: By that time the war was going great guns, I got $17 a day down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where are you talking about, down at the mill?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No, down at the steel smith.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh really? Just working for them? Yeah, well when I went up to the mill, then I got a dollar six bits an hour, didn't I?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah, I think so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were you doing for steel smiths?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh, sowing sacks on the cow mine.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah, that was big money. I worked for Berta Walner down there after steel smiths were done. I was getting $17 a day, but then that was war, at times.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you do the same thing for Walner, Saxon?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No, I did Saxon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had you done that before?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh, you bet. I bet lots of them. When I was just a young kid I used to do that. I'd go out to those stationeries and sew sacks for 35, 40 days at a time. That was not new to me, the combine was. That was my first experience on a combine, but it didn't make any difference. Sowing a sack was the same. The way you sat and put it on that moving machine bothered for a couple of hours, and then after that you didn't pay any attention to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean it took a while to get used to the position?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: The jiggle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was a pretty skilled job on those crews, wasn't it?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: A pretty hard, tough job to get. Semi skilled.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Any farmer, prettoneer, could do it, but the farmer had to take care of his own business. He couldn't go and help somebody else. The days of changing labor were out. Help with two damn skiffs.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: The guy ahead of me out there at Steelsmith didn't know how to sew sacks. If he got behind, he'd just throw the sack off the side, didn't sew it, and dump it off. He dumped off about four or five, and Frank said, no more of that. He just shut the machine down and sent Kelly to town to get a man. She got into the range with me, and I went out there. I worked for him several years. I got along just fine.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Finally, one day, the combine wheel jumped into a sharp ditch. Just as I pulled that 150 -pound sack of peas up over my knee, it tore the muscle loose in my back somewhere.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I was in terrible pain. It took a long time to get over that. But they were insured, and not enough to eat. That's all we needed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you working in the mills at the same time?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Back then, or that was later?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I had worked in mills, yeah, all my life. Or potlatch, you had. I had worked in mills all my life, off and on. But during that time, I wasn't, I had enough hair to almost keep me busy and almost do the job. And then I'd get my harvest done here earlier, while I'd go out and hire out to somebody else for a few days that were remaining.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this place pretty much ready to work and farm when you got it, or did you wind up having a lot to do to be able to operate?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: This place had 25 acres cleared on it. No fences, no buildings, excepting that little house out there.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I don't know how we did it, but we did it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And now there's 125 acres on it, and somebody else is farming it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And all but 22 acres of that were cleared up with the horses, cleared up and plowed and broke and farmed with the horses. Walt Littler cleared up 22 acres, one step up here and one over on the other side of the place. And then, no, well, then we got our own captain cleared up some more up there. That'll come later. But right now it's down to 125 acres of it in crop.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you cleared with the horses, was it much the way that your father cleared his place?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much difference on that?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No, just the same. There was no change there. The change come when they got bulldozers and cats and big plows.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Horse days, that just, as long as there's horses, it couldn't change. It's just one way to do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Stump puller?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: We had a stump puller over there, but I never hear. No.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Powder, crack them with the powder, pull them with block and lines. Block and tackle you'd call it probably.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Cable, big blocks, and then...

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: We didn't take all the stumps out the first time around, not by any means. We went around them, over them. We left them there to break our rigging up in the future years and to sure as hell did it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But, reduce the best you could. Get by with it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: You could clear up two acres a year, you were doing awful good.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And they come in with a bulldozer in later years and clean up two acres in an hour.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: An hour? Really?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah.  Well, it wasn't all done very good. Well, we had... We had chopped the big timber down and cut it into wood and...

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And passed their sheep on it until they killed all the brush.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And that final shot that Walt made here, the 22 acres, heck, he done that in about 10 hours. With the dozer, small dozer at that.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, didn't Hans do some too?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Uh -uh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did Littler come in here?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, it was in the 50s, so...

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: About 55 or 6, somewhere along there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So when you started, you were only raising crop on about 25 acres?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: That's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And enlarging that every year by a couple acres?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: A.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah. Yeah, you couldn't work at that enlarging a place steady. You had to take a day here and an hour there.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And then two days someplace else. You had to make a living. Whether you made it making cedar poles or whether you made it cutting wood or working for saw mill or some other farmer, any way you could make a dollar.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And then we're going to turn up another little bit just spare time.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: First thing, we went back here to look at the place, really, you know, look it over. I think we went in about that deep of dust.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About a foot deep, huh?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Just about.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Had a big wind that had all gone over there or something.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Kind of think of it, we did have a lot of wind blowing dust. See the next few years. Well, when this land came out of the timber, it was fluffy, loose, red colored.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And you had to farm it three or four years before it settled down. V, what it is now. It's a chuck full of tree roots and grass roots.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: They used to call it tamarack soil. Different than anything we'd ever seen, but it produced pretty good crops.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Right away or after it had been sown?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yes it did. It didn't do too bad, especially if you put it in hay, which we did.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Grass, clover, alfalfa, anything to get hay. I didn't like wheat, never ever liked wheat, and I never will. I didn't like it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: One reason why we objected to wheat over there at Harvard, two times at least, it froze out. Well, one time it froze and one time we got that striped frost.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I had a crop there that looked like it was going to make 50 bushel and the frost hit it in July when it was right in bloom. And it made 15 bushel of damn poor hog feed.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And then another year, the next year, the following year, had about 40 or 45 acres of pretty good looking fall wheat. And that striped frost, no that was spring wheat, that was striped frost hit it. And it made about 15 bushel of hog feed. Not good hog feed either. Didn't get any kind of a price on it at all.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But, we'd get some pretty fair crops on part of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you went for raising hay and keeping your own stock here from the beginning?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: That's right. We didn't want any wheat. We just put wheat enough on it to condition the ground and get it so it would raise a crop of hay. Get it level enough so you thought you could mow it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But I never left it in grain over a year or two and then I'd put it in hay. Hay was what I wanted.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: That's always been my crop. I don't know, I had good luck with it. And the stock was better for me than grain. They were good to me. Always made a little bit of headway.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: We never did have a bump crop on this place by frost or rust or parasites or anything of the sort. We were just lucky here. We never had a thing to hurt us that way.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Always had a good crop of hay and a fairly good crop of grain.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: About 1948, it rained and rained and rained. We had the wheat bound and shocked and it just kept on raining. Every day I'd go out and look at it and that wheat was just on top of those shocks, just as green as could be. Beautiful. Every kernel on top was sprouted and wheat got up that high on top of the shocks. Fortunately, I had it insured.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Henry Botcher came out from Moscow and afraid didn't give me about 75 % loss.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was leaving and I said, Henry, what will I do with that stuff?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: He said, do whatever you want to. It's yours. You can burn it. You can plow it under.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: He suggested things that I could do with it. But he didn't say anything about hauling it in the barn and feeding it. I didn't either.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: So I'd bring in load and I'd spread it all over the barn. It was wet and green. And then the next day I'd get mother load and bring it in. You know those pigs and sheep and cows had more fun thrashing that crop than I ever had running it through a separator. And it made me a little money. I don't know whether I was supposed to do that or not, but I did it. Then the neighbor down here, he didn't have any stock.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When I got through with mine, he said, do you want some more?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I said, sure.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: So I hauled his wheat crop up here and put it away. I had to stack most of that outside, but they ate it and they loved it and they got fat on it. Although it was absolutely worthless, you couldn't sell it. It just was worthless to anybody else.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I went to Lawson and bought just a few more sheep and I think I bought an extra fig or two. I didn't have money enough to do me any good, but I got a few.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I'm pretty sure I had a time around here. They ate it. The extra stock, you bought, ate it. Yeah. I'm going to have to go out and see if I can round up another tape out of my car.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: First over here, it seems like with that little house, our guy re -assigned to me once. Sometimes when we had company, somebody had to stand against the door to hold its shot.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, the jury used to get for them, but we made it. Boy, look at the picture of all the kids in that one picture. You had a big family for that. Yeah. Of course, the old girl was married, but when the last one came, so... ...one whole room and boarded up a porch.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, they didn't use them both at the same time. When the other room was ready to fill with the porch, they moved off the porch. So that was kind of an airy... Well, Art described it pretty good. He said we were a little bit crowded, but he said we made do. You had the same situation. Some very... Oh. Yeah, I think that was a pretty common situation with the older houses and the big families. These homesteaders out on the prairie out here all had the same situation. Well, some of them had big houses to start with.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To start with?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No, very few of them. They got them pretty suddenly, but they had to get a crop or two before they had the money to do it. That house was standing when you came. Yeah. Yeah, but there were three rooms and a back porch. And I guess the back porch was boarded in, but it had to be some work done because it was real cold. Yeah. You put on felt paper and more... Well, as they got bigger and couldn't sleep three in a bed, why, we'd finally struggle around and get another room added on so they could have at least two in a bed. The most two in a bed, however it works.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did you divide up the house when you were first there, the space? Did you have one room to yourself?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah. Yeah, it was a little bit small, a very small bedroom. Well, there were two bedrooms. One of the girls and the boys weren't as big. You see, the two oldest girls were the oldest. And Boyd and Richard were, and that one, we had an Edgar. So, they were small enough to pack around. Oh, yeah, they were crowded, but we made do. Well, we got that porch fixed up for them pretty early and made a nice room for the boys. The girls had one. And then we built that back on, and then the boys had lots of room and a nice situation out of that. And then we started this.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: That's another beauty of being ignorant. If I hadn't known what it's going to take in work and hours and dollars, I never would have started this house. I wouldn't have the nerve. Well, the other one was just kind of on the way down, so there wasn't any foundation under it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: It was kind of a have -to deal. Yeah. Of course, I suppose you could have put a foundation under it. You wouldn't have had anything when you got done. That house was built in the 70s, the first original part of it. It was put up without any studs between the walls. I don't know how they did it. Box. Box. Must have been the top and bottom must have been nailed.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, in these pamphlets from Whitman County, there were lots of those old cabins built that way. They'd make a foundation or a start, and put a tube of four at the bottom and a tube of four at the top, and then they nailed these one -by -twelve boards just like that up.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And there'd be a two -inch air space in there, so they matted the outside with one -by -fours. And then they put in their inner wall and put wallpaper over it. And then the lumber would shrink and the wallpaper would crack and the wind would blow through and the snow would blow through, and they'd patch that up and paper it again.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I don't know which one of it was, telling them about paper, and it was the first time with newspapers. Then they got prosperous and got a roll or two of felt paper and they papered it with that. Then they got some fancy wallpaper and papered it with that. That's three layers. And every year they'd paper it again to clean the walls up. So they had about 15 or 20 layers of paper on there that began to get pretty warm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about your family's place when you grew up?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, we did about the same thing over at Harvard.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But here,

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: we only papered out over there about three or four times, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Over here?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Probably not that many. It was comfortable. It was pretty good. Pretty good. You're laughing. You don't quite find that. Well, the first time we tried to paper it after, it was pretty good paper on there when we came. Then it got to being dirty and torn a little here and there. Somebody gave us some paper, and it wasn't very good paper. The first one, we started out bright and shiny one morning. He got it all glued and everything ready to put it over his head, and his head went right through it. I started to laugh, and I couldn't stop laughing.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: That's the wonder. After that, it seemed to go pretty good.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: It's the one who didn't kill me.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I guess he saw how funny it was himself. He had a few laughs himself. For kids nowadays, it seems like the space that each kid or each person requires is so much more than it was not that long ago. I can't imagine two kids in bed, two or three beds in the bedroom.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Nowadays, you're supposed to have a bed of their own in a bedroom.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, they finally got it. Let's see, it had to get down to Grandkidsville before we got it that way.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Although it was not too bad. Two of them in the bed, they were comfortable. It didn't start out any other way. Why, they liked it all right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you envision when you started to think about building this house, if not the house in its present form? What were you thinking of?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I mean, it's a beautiful house. I always figured you just imagine from the beginning that you were going to build it up like this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, we had wanted, up there at Harvard, if we had built, we were going to build a log house, you think?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah. We wanted it, anyway. But we thought when we started this house that it was going to be so big, so much bigger than the other one. Well, which it is. It's about twice as big or more. But still, when any of the kids come home, we kind of divide them up with the rest of the family to make room for them. Well, I guess you get another family in there or two.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, it's going to fill it up, okay?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And it does. So when I went and came home for the reunion, we didn't think of anything else. We just let them go to Edgards and, well, I guess that was all. Well, we had room for, there were two up there, three up there, and two up there, and two here. Oh, they got in, maybe a little crowded, but they made do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, were you thinking about the house, about this scale, about the way it turned out before you started building it?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, I never would have had any idea that it would take so much room to hold a family. A few second generations in there, third.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But we had this plan. We planned it for years, even where the windows and doors were going to be. When we started in on it, we had a pretty good idea what was going to end up.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But it took quite a while, about ten years, but I didn't see whether there was any rush.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Everybody says, why don't you get a loan and get a contract?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh, heck, I don't want a loan because you've got to pay them back. I'd sooner have my own work in it. It may take a little longer, but it was mine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year did you start building? We started this thing in 50, didn't we?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah, cutting love.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that the way you proceeded?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: You started by... Well, first to cut the logs and got them all ready. And then a gypo mill came in, up here on the next place, and I gave him a share of the logs to Millmont.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And he took his share, as much of mine as he could get, but it wasn't too bad. I kept getting a little gain on it all the time. I'd get him, put a log on the carriage, and think, well, that's a good cabin log. And first thing I knew, he had it thawed up in Daines -Lumber, so that would go to Lowston. But we kept at it and finally got enough. And I had a big load of selects pulled out, like those over there with no knots, no stains, no nothing. I went to Moscow for something.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, when I came back, that was gone. I had to start almost all over again, but next time I held on to that select stuff, when I got it, I brought it home.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And finally got through.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So this guy's what we call a rip -off today, huh?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah, I did, that's all. And they were big kids, they were great school.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yes.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: You don't have to use any names here. We can leave it at that. Well, I think some of them might still remember. Well. Pretty long time.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, what you were doing was you were getting your logs sawed in half and then getting some, and getting select, trying to get the best lumber for the paneling you can. Well, I wanted a good, I wanted my first sir of the better grades, yes. I thought I was entitled to that, and I wound up with, I'm satisfied.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, these exterior, the logs, are they cut in half or are they whole logs? Oh, these logs?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: The ones on the outside and the outside. They're square, they're squared on three sides. I see. Yeah, two. And made an eight inch square with the round side to go out. And then we didn't have to notch any corners, we just cribbed it. Just played it like that. And another log went in here, and then another log went over the top and cut this corner again. Made a substantial corner, and then we put the wall in a wall like this, and there'd be about five, sixty penny spikes. We bored a hole halfway through the log and drove that nail in there. Until a head hit the hole we'd bored, and that put it halfway through the next log. There were eight inch nails and went four inches in each log. And it don't seem to be given any place, it's staying there.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Stay a long time, nothing happens to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, how did the work progress the first year you got the logs cut?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yes, and the next year we got them sawed. And then during that summer the kids dug the basement with a team of horses. They had a time of it, but they did her. I was working at the mill then, up at Moscow.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And we'd put up the, on Saturdays and Sundays or any time we could get where we'd put up these walls.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And got them up where they're going to be, and then, I don't know, we got that roof up there some way. Next, it wasn't closed at the end, but we had the roof on it. That made it so we could work when the mill set down, and then we could work all winter in here. We did. And all that lumber was piled here in the middle of the floor. There was no division there.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And we had a stove here. And try and get it dry. And we thought I had it dry. Put it up and it'd shrink. Not too bad, but it shrunk. And I'd always thought that I'd like to take it down and put them tight, but I never will. That's all right. You did this for here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About took ten years, so what?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: From start to finish.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it ten years before you moved in?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No. No, we... We moved in in 1956. We're six years before we moved in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what remained to be done at that point?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh, these walls need to be finished. The bedrooms need to be lined, and cabinets built, and fireplace built.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And we didn't get the fireplace built until 62.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But we just kept the tekken.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I got money enough to get the wire.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And I asked a contractor to come in and put that wire up while the building was still pretty much of a skeleton.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And he wanted to contract to do the whole thing. Furnish the wire and put it in. But I couldn't do it. These walls weren't done. They couldn't put the lamps in. And I didn't know the ceiling was floored up there, but it wasn't floored underneath. The ceiling wasn't there. But anyway, he didn't want any part of it because I wouldn't contract to do the whole thing, so he just passed it up. And then I didn't know anything about electricity, but I asked everybody I could see what to do about that wire, and they all advised get the heaviest wire you can possibly get. For that purpose. And I did. And before this wall went up, I put the wire in there. It comes in over here and down in the basement, and then comes up the wall and into there and over here. And, oh, I got by with it. But he wanted me to borrow the money, give him a contract, and go ahead. And I couldn't see it. He couldn't see it. He couldn't see it my way. I couldn't see it his.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Just as well, we got by.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you ever built a house before this one, Mike?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh, not alone. I'd helped Nance on his. I helped Dean High on his. I helped Burgers on theirs. Even as a kid, I used to get in a lot of house raising and things like that. Oh, yeah, I had a little... enough to know what to do, how to do it, but I wouldn't be as fast as an expert or a carpenter.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But I had a pretty fair idea.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: The biggest help I got while I was going to prep would be high school. I took a couple of three semesters in cabinet making, or just woodwork. But I learned how to use the tools and what they were made for and why they were made and so on. That would give me an idea that I could do it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Could and did.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, the old instructor down there, C. Roberts, said, if you can build a perfect box, four sides of it, and make it perfect, he said, you can build anything there is out of wood. I didn't quite get that box perfect, but I got it by enough to get a grade. And then I took the knowledge to work in here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What prep school, prep was this? Where was this at?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Pullman. That college used to have what they called a prep school there for places that didn't have high schools, we could go in there and take high school courses.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: You could get about anything you wanted to. Basics, it was high school, that's all there was money to. The only thing is that high school never, never was as hard as that. No, they crowded us. I took high school about the same time he took that. And we didn't have the assignments nor the kinds of books that they had in there. Or any time during high school. They crowded us harder. The course was supposed to, we were supposed to get our, what's ordinary, high school in four years. But we had to get that in three. It didn't give us four years. So the assignments were pretty big, and the load was heavy. And leave me they pushed us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that good or bad? Good or bad to have to work at?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: For a kid that was ordinary intelligence, it was probably good. But I was so goddamn dumb it was just all I could do to get by. Well, I don't think that because as I say, I took high school about the same time you did that. And I've seen your books and we never had anything like it in high school. And the courses then were probably a lot harder then than they are now. So there's no comparison. I know they pushed us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you live in Pullman?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you live?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I don't know whether the conservatory, music conservatory is where it used to be or not. But we lived just across the street from it down over a bank.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Student housing? Pardon? Do you live with other students? How many kids were with us?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh, there was four of us batched. Oh. In a regular house. In a little one room shack. It was about 14 or 16 feet wide and about 20 feet long. Kitchen in one end, dining area in the middle and beds were double bunks in the back. We did our own cooking.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And I worked all the time. Well, I'd say about, I'd bring there four hours a day and set up half the night.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Studying, I hope. Oh, well, I got by.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where'd you work? Huh? Where'd you work?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh, in most cows out at the dairy. One year I was the barn boss out there. I used to have about, oh, 25, 30 head of horses and I took care of those horses night and morning. I'd get over there about three o 'clock in the morning and feed them and curry them.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And harness what they were going to use that day. And then somebody else come along and cleaned the barn out. I didn't have to do that, but it took all the time I had. And then I had to go back over in the evening and harness them, curry them, feed them and water them.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Take care of them. I loved it, that part of it. I think I would have stayed over there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Over in Pullman?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No, in the horse barn.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Sounds better than the one room shack. Well, at least he would have had more time to study. Well, we worked to start studying just pretty conscientiously. We knew that it was up to us. There was no problem. We might go to a dance once a week. Now, very rarely have I ever went to a show. Unless there was something up on the campus there. We used to go to the occasional ball games, but not too many. There just wasn't time for it. We did try to get to a dance spot once a week. All of us liked to dance.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: There's all those girls around there that were glad to get a date.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: There was a bunch of these boys. You see, now, like at that time,

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Spangyl didn't have a high school. And there was Boyd Lambert and Dolly and truth to the others from Spangyl there. So the district up there paid tuition. We didn't have to pay any tuition. We had to buy our books. And our boarding room.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And clothes. That was about it. But, you know, with the few hours that I worked every day, and that was seven days a week,

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I could make and save during the summer about three, four, and one year, five hundred dollars.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Now, a lot of that was earned at a dollar a day. In harvest, I could get three.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But, that, and what I could make, I think I got about fifty cents an hour over there.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: So I made about two dollars a day. Financially, got by fine. Good. Let me do it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But, I never was any great deal ahead in my studies. Well, they shouldn't have charged you one cent for books.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did they?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Washington paid all the books, papers, pencils, at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was your family living at the time?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh, Harvard. No school. It was a great school. There was a high school in Potlach, but we wanted to go down here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why? Because you figured it was better? Better school?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, yes, we thought that. We wouldn't have had a chance to work, probably. No.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: They had more to choose from. I was agriculture. I wanted

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: animal husbandry and things like that. And I couldn't get it. Unless I went to them. That was my main influence. I could get a broader choice of things. However, they crowded us with algebra and history and geometry and all

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: of that. They just made us take that and you get one or two classes in each semester of something like botany or horticulture. You could have got that in high school. Well, I know, but animal husbandry, I always got a semester of that in there.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Every semester.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it your decision to go, Glenn? Or was it your parents?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No, it was mine. It was strictly mine, as far as I was concerned. And then my neighbor kids, it was theirs too. Their parents encouraged it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: One of them wanted to take a train to be a teacher. Another one wanted to study more or less languages, Spanish and things like that.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And you couldn't specialize in high school, but then they, all of us, did all right. After we got all there, we went into our line of work and we did all right at it. Well, I think that if you'd taken high school potlatch, you probably would have had the same thing, except you wouldn't have had a chance to work. Well, I couldn't have got animal husbandry or feeds and seeds or crops and seeds and things like that. But, yeah, that's right. But it was about six or one and a half dozen, although I think we've been just well off either place.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Those days, Harvard and, or two miles beyond Harvard, and potlatch were a long way apart. There wasn't any cars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Guess there were cars, but who had them?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Very few cars then. And it's too far for a horse to make the trip every day. Yeah.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: His sisters stayed in town and worked their heads off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where? Where I worked? On Knob Hill or where?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No. They worked on the other hill.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But they worked. Yes. Hill was, they worked. Darn right, they worked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did they do? Did they work?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh, it was work. Take care of the kids. Old heifers, they'd leave the dinner dishes and bed making and floor sleeping and everything else for those kids to do after they got home from school.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: They took a hell of an advantage of them. Sounds like Hannah Ut talking about she did the same thing in potlatch. Yes, she did. My sister did the same thing in Seattle. Carol did the same thing at Moscow when she went to university. It's just a common thing that slough off onto the... Why, those women wouldn't do a hand's turn that they could possibly leave for those kids to do after they got home. It's always been that way and always will be, I guess.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Now it's a TV to look at. They can sit and look at it. To put up with it, the kids must have wanted to get a certain amount of education. Well, they did. They did. They figured it would be benefited enough to make it worthwhile to do it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But the... If I was to do it over again, I'd still do it the same way. But I wouldn't try to go through in three years. I would make them lower that load. So if I wanted to, it could take five years. You could do it now, but you couldn't do it then. They just piled it under. You had so many hours when you registered and that was it. So it was really a struggle to keep up with all that stuff. I couldn't. I just had an awful time.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Continue around the pleasure. I'm not sure I'm going to go through them.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I don't know. Now you probably read more history than most anybody I know.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: So it didn't destroy your love of learning. No. No, I think personally, the more they study and read, the more they want to. Most of the people that I've known can't get enough degrees and stuff. I know one woman used to be over here near the church. She studied and gone to school for, I imagine, 15 years at least, if her life, maybe more. If I had kept at it, probably I would have been a lot more apt to go on than I ever... ...your exposure to that. Because you once described to me at Camp 11 what that was like.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you describe that to me again, what Camp 11 was like when you saw it?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, the living conditions. Yeah, when they were pretty bad. Well, the bunkhouse was 30 by, I imagine, 75 feet. And there was a row of double bunks everywhere around the wall. And each bunk, one above the other.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I put about, oh, must have been 75 men in that one great big room. Had a stove in each end.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And we'd all come in, soft and wet, night after night after night. Well, the only place you could get those clothes dry was to hang them up to the ceiling. With 75 coats, 75 pair of socks, 75 suits of underwear, and 75 suits of overalls or Malone pants. Right in there they... You know, there is people, I bet you they swear that it stinks.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: The goddamn lousy bunkhouses, somebody, they'd get it cleaned up. Maybe once every two or three years. Then somebody'd come in with a batch of ice and go through the whole damn bunkhouse. Bed bugs.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh, hell. They lived a little bit, hell, the farmers used to take better care of the hogs than potlice took care of the men.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: The cook shack was the same size building.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And great long tables right down the middle of it, two rows of them. And there was one place where they did a good job. Anything that you could imagine, they had steak for breakfast, they had bacon for breakfast, hotcakes for breakfast, fruit, fresh milk.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Pastry, baked goods, man, it was just tremendous. Oh, oh, I can eat that. After you'd been there about a month, the cake tasted just like the potatoes and the steak. I swear it did. They said the reason for that was you got the wrinkles out of your belly and...

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Okay. By that time you were getting particular, but the cooks were good. They were tremendous. And the potlice furnished them anything they wanted to cook, it just didn't matter what it was. We might not have been here, honey, soon enough. Fresh meat used to come in every day as far as I know. However, earlier than that when they didn't have the transportation, it used to get pretty rank.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When was it you were in there, in Camp 11?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, it would have to be 1918,

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I think. So the cooking was good and the living conditions were atrocious. There was news they could make them clean. I think you're not getting...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you hear what he said?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I said the cooking was good, the food was good, but the living conditions were terrible. Well, that's right. That's funny, they had them both good cooking and bad living, but I've heard it before. Well, that's the way it was. That bunkhouse was terrible. However, right at that time when these IWWs come in, and then it was seven -manned to a marion shack. And we had a room as big as this one, a little 30 feet long. And I believe they were 10 or 12 feet wide.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And there'd be two bunks across from each other, in the end that's four men. And then two bunks about halfway up. And then one bunk being one corner, or the other. And there was always an empty bunk, they never let them fill it. But it was for company or camping, inspection, or such and such. But then, by that time, we had electric lights, bathroom,

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: showers. There were no towels, but showers. And you could wash your clothes nights if you wanted to. And they even had a special car where you could go and play cards, or just sit and talk, or read. Generally read near a bunk because it was quiet.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But it just changed from terrible to pretty darn good. They're just in one year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that year 1918?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: 18 and 19, yes. It changed right in there, I don't remember exactly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you attributed it to the IWWs?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Yeah, the labor trouble forced it. They fought it, the company fought it in every way that they could. But it just seemed like they couldn't put it over.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: God, they used to fight that.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, they got a kick out of our... And they didn't mention any names, but I knew that this guy that got so white in the face, that was Jim O 'Connell.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But they finally worked in. And instead of... There's something wrong there because the IWWs in 1918, and General O 'Connell came about in 1934. He didn't come until 1930 or a way up from there. Well, there was more organizing that went on later. Art was talking about CIO, which was during the 30s. Yeah, well, you see, they had kind of a... Well, first they had the 4Ls. That was the company's union.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they try to make you join that one?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh, yeah, they had to join it. They wanted to work there. After you joined it, all you did was pay your dues. They got no benefit from it. But it kept the unions out.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And then there was a long period in there that didn't have anything.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And then finally, the time that Art was talking about was after the Depression.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, then they did form a union, and the guys like him put it over.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I admired him for putting it over and then helping them, although after they got in there, he couldn't belong to it. Because they had him in a supervisor's job.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But for them to beat him out of his retirement money over that deal was typically potlatch. That's just the way they worked. He wasn't alone on that. Malcolm Greer and numerous ones got the same thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For union activity? Or for other unions?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Punishment was even sympathizing with them.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Well, potlatch and all other companies did not want this union. They knew that they were going to have to pay their wage, and they didn't want to. That's breaking into their profits. But they finally had to.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Did you ever come...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were working there at 18, and did you have any contact with the IWWs?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Or with the company propaganda against them?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Not particularly, no. I just had a job to do, and I did it.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I thought that IWW was working before our time. Well, we were. That's a fact. I mean, they kind of make it work. The IWWs came, and then they kind of quieted them down, but at the same time, they got the eight hour day pretty well through, and they did get clean bunk houses.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And then they kind of died down. The mines were organized, and they stayed organized, but the mills didn't. And then they got these four IWWs, which were just a farce. Took a couple of dollars out of every check. And then there wasn't anything. And then the unions came back in and organized and put it over, and they've had a pretty good deal ever since.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But it was a long time struggle.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But let's... Used to can these people over sympathy or making remarks or things like that. They'd can them. They might hire them back in a couple of weeks, but they'd can them. And sometimes when they can them, they'd leave and go down to Bend or down to Lewiston or down to Longview. There's a lot of places they could go if they had money enough to get out of town.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Most of them didn't have money enough to get out unless they went out by night. You know, Windmire took his wife, and each of them took a kid by the fist, and they walked out. They had to. They couldn't get a job.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: But... I knew they came back to town walking, but I didn't know they walked out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They left and came back, didn't they?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: About 1930 or somewhere along that.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: You run in Windmire.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was the second time he was in town or the first?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No, it was the first time. I never seen him the second time he was in there. No, him and I had a little disagreement, but I got away with it. Yeah, he didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you work in the woods very much going in or what?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: No, no, no, no. I was in 1926 or 7 when I did. Twenty -six. I say up until about 1926 or 25 along in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did I?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I put a lot of time in the woods. And then, 1935, I think it was, we were up at Harvard, and I went back into Woods Inn. Summers and spare time. And still did my farming.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And then, by that time, I figured I'd almost had enough of the woods. I was going to work inside if I worked in the winter, so I started in these sawmills. However, I worked 1927 and 1928 in that boiler room over there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In Potlash?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And I worked there, and I worked down in the dark in 1929.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And then, October, I quit, and we bought that dairy outfit. We shut down. The mill shut down in 1929, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were working in the woods, what kind of work did you usually do?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: A pair of horses, if it was humanly possible.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did I know?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: That's what you were going to say. But, however, sometimes I'd had, I worked on the landing up at Camp 2 at Collins.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Season.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And, way back, while I was still in grade school, I used to saw for... ...just hot laying...

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: ...can't even remember the other fellow's name. There was logging on that Lenhart place over there we used to saw during the summertime there. That's all the sawing I ever did to a mount or anything. Well, but...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: If I could get behind an old team of horses, that's what I wanted, and that's what I did. And, believe me, I was just good enough that if I wanted a job, all I had to do was go into camp and ask for it. They might turn a dozen away, but I never did get turned away. I'd go into the main office up here in Bowell, and tell Noggle I'd like to get a team someplace, and he'd say, well, peer, or go up there, and I'd have a team.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: So what you knew how to do from handling a team on the farm held you in good stead when you wanted to handle a team in the woods. That's right. I'd just grown up with them.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I worked one year for Shaddick. He could have had 50 other guys if he'd have wanted them, but he wanted me. And one year I worked with Corey. Same deal. He could have had any amount of them, but he wanted me. And, Queener, after I started, I worked for him at NEVThat's where I worked for Shaddick. And as long as Queener would log in if I wanted a job with him, I had a team of horses to drive.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: Oh, I like that.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I still like oars.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I put in all the time in the sawmills until they made that against the law. I put steam in the room there in the potlots, boiler room. And then later I went over to Carvscall and run the engine over there for a while.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: And then everything went to electricity. There just wasn't any steam, and so I went to edging in.

speaker: Glen or Agnes Gilder
words: I never wanted to work for the little man on the totem pole. I wanted to work up there where he was making pretty good money. It took just a little bit of skill. I tried to get it somewhere.
